title: System Design Core Tradeoffs
description: High-frequency system-design tradeoffs with concise interview-ready framing.

cards.question: How do you choose between SQL and NoSQL?
cards.shortAnswer: Choose based on access patterns and domain guarantees, not on trend.
cards.expectedAnswer: I choose based on access patterns and the guarantees the domain needs. If the problem requires integrity, transactions, and strong relationships, SQL is usually the natural fit. If the schema changes often and the system is more document-oriented or distribution-heavy, NoSQL can be a better fit. The key is not choosing by trend but by the shape of the workload and the risk of the domain.
keyPoints: schema flexibility and document shape may favor NoSQL
cards.learnMore:
This answer gets stronger when you tie the database choice back to business risk. Payments, ledgers, and domains with strict consistency rules usually lean SQL because relationships, constraints, and transactions matter a lot.

Content-heavy or document-shaped systems may lean NoSQL when the access pattern is simple and flexibility matters more than relational guarantees. The point is not that one is modern and the other is old. The point is the workload.
commonTraps: saying NoSQL scales and SQL does not
answering without talking about domain guarantees
followUps: When would you denormalize in a SQL system?
What is the operational cost of sharding?

cards.question: How do you think about monolith vs microservices?
cards.shortAnswer: I default to a monolith until there is real pressure from scale, domain boundaries, or team autonomy.
cards.expectedAnswer: I usually start with a monolith because it keeps delivery, debugging, and operations simpler. Microservices become attractive when there is real pressure from domain boundaries, independent scaling needs, or team autonomy. The main mistake is distributing complexity too early before the system has earned it.
keyPoints: microservices need real pressure to justify complexity
cards.learnMore:
A lot of weak answers make this sound ideological. A better answer is operational. Monoliths reduce coordination cost, local debugging is easier, and transactional workflows are often simpler.

Microservices only start paying rent when you have clear domain seams, different scaling profiles, or teams that truly need to ship independently. Otherwise you just move complexity from code structure into networking, deployment, and observability.
cards.exampleCode:
// Monolith: one deployable service
/api/orders
/api/payments
/api/inventory

// Microservices: separate deployables with explicit boundaries
orders-service
payments-service
inventory-service
commonTraps: treating microservices as a maturity badge
followUps: What is the first pain signal that a monolith is not enough?
How would you split a monolith safely?
